steps.prompt: Which words form the main verb?

hints.reprobaverunt: 'Reprobaverunt' is a finite verb but it is in the subordinate relative clause
hints.aedificantes: 'Aedificantes' is a present active participle meaning 'the builders'
hints.Lapidem: 'Lapidem' is a noun meaning 'stone' in the accusative

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'factus est' translates to?

hints.to make / to do: 'To make' is 'facere' (active) — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri' (to become)
hints.to carry: 'To carry' is 'ferre' — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri'
hints.to say: 'To say' is 'dicere' — 'factus est' is from 'fio, fieri'

steps.prompt: What tense and mood is 'factus est'?
steps.reveal: factus est = has become (3rd sing. perf.)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'fit' — 'factus est' is perfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'factus erat' — 'factus est' is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'factus sit' — 'factus est' is indicative

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the active parts of 'facio' — 'fio' serves as its passive
hints.fingo, fingere, finxi, fictum: These are the parts of 'fingo' (to form/fashion)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'fero' (to carry)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'reprobant' — 'reprobaverunt' with -averunt is perfect
hints.future perfect: Future perfect would be 'reprobaverint' — 'reprobaverunt' is perfect

hints.probo, probare, probavi, probatum: These are the parts of 'probo' (to test/approve) — 'reprobare' is re- + probare
hints.reprehendo, reprehendere, reprehendi, re: These are the parts of 'reprehendo' (to blame/criticize)
hints.removeo, removere, removi, remotum: These are the parts of 'removeo' (to remove)

steps.reveal: Lapidem = the stone (acc., direct object of 'reprobaverunt' and fronted for emphasis)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'lapis' — 'Lapidem' with -em is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'lapidis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'lapidis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'lapidis' ends in -is

steps.reveal: in caput anguli = into the head/chief of the corner (acc., 'in' + acc. = motion/result)

hints.nominative: 'Caput' looks the same in nominative, but 'in' + acc. indicates result/transformation
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'capite' — 'in' + acc. = resulting in / becoming
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'capitis' — 'caput' is accusative after 'in'

hints.nominative plural: Nominative plural 'anguli' looks the same, but context requires genitive modifying 'caput'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'angulo' — 'anguli' is genitive singular
hints.vocative: Vocative plural 'anguli' looks the same, but this is not direct address

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'anguli' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'anguli' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'anguli' ends in -i

steps.reveal: aedificantes = the builders (present participle used as a noun)

hints.attributive adjective: An attributive adjective would modify a stated noun — 'aedificantes' stands alone as a substantive
hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute requires ablative case — 'aedificantes' is nominative
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'aedificandus' — 'aedificantes' is a present active participle

translation: The stone which the builders rejected has become the cornerstone
